elements.sent-2411-001: Lumped-element inductors are an integral component in the circuit QED toolbox.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, it is challenging to build inductors that are simultaneously compact, linear and low-loss with standard approaches that either rely on the geometric inductance of superconducting thin films or on the kinetic inductance of Josephson junctions arrays.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we overcome this challenge by utilizing the high kinetic inductance offered by superconducting granular aluminum (grAl).
elements.sent-2411-004: We demonstrate lumped-element inductors with a few nH of inductance that are up to $100$ times more compact than inductors built from pure aluminum (Al).
elements.sent-2411-005: To characterize the properties of these linear inductors, we first report on the performance of lumped-element resonators built entirely out of grAl with sheet inductances varying from $30-320$\,pH/sq and self-Kerr non-linearities of $0.2-20\,\mathrm{Hz/photon}$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Further, we demonstrate ex-situ integration of these grAl inductors into hybrid resonators with Al or tantalum (Ta) capacitor electrodes without increasing total internal losses.
elements.sent-2411-007: Interestingly, the measured internal quality factors systematically decrease with increasing room-temperature resistivity of the grAl film for all devices, indicating a trade-off between compactness and internal loss.
elements.sent-2411-008: For our lowest resistivity grAl films, we measure quality factors reaching $3.5 \times 10^6$ for the all-grAl devices and $4.5 \times 10^6$ for the hybrid grAl/Ta devices, similar to state-of-the-art quantum circuits.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our loss analysis suggests that the surface loss factor of grAl is similar to that of pure Al for our lowest resistivity films, while the increasing losses with resistivity could be explained by increasing conductor loss in the grAl film.
elements.sent-2411-010: Superconducting architectures for quantum information processing are growing increasingly complex\,\cite{Arute2019,Chen2021,Jurcevic_2021,Bravyi_outlook_2022,Xu_2023,acharya2024quantumerrorcorrectionsurface , as individual circuit components are being optimized to perform specific functions like storage\,\cite{Reagor_2013,Reagor_2016,Axline_2016,ganjam_surpassing_2024 , gates\,\cite{Niskanen_2007_coupler,Chen_2014,Moskalenko2022,Ding_2023_fluxonium,Marxer_2023transmon_coupler or logical readout\,\cite{Jeffrey_2014,Walter_2017,Sunada_2024,Hazra_2024 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Depending on the desired functionality, different obstacles need to be overcome.
elements.sent-2411-012: These range from improving the noise resilience of qubits\,\cite{Pop2014,Hassani2023 , activating fast operations between circuit modes\,\cite{Xu_2020_coupler,Chapman_2023,Lu2023 and making them more resilient to strong driving\,\cite{Verney_2019,gusenkova_quantum_2021,Geisert_2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: An interesting avenue to address these problems is to provide a linear inductive shunt for the nonlinear Josephson junction (JJ)\,\cite{Mooij_1999, Manucharyan_2009,Koch_2009,yan2020engineering,Ye_2021_Quarton,Kalacheva_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: This shunt confines the otherwise periodic Josephson potential while also enabling in-situ tunability of the circuit parameters with external magnetic fields\,\cite{Frattini_2017,Vool_2018,Lescanne2020_ATS .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Ideally, the inductor has a small footprint, making the circuit compact while simultaneously reducing the number of low frequency parasitic modes.
elements.sent-2411-016: Further, it has low internal loss, and is easy to fabricate and integrate with other circuit components (Fig.\,\ref{Fig1 a).}
elements.sent-2411-017: While adding such an ideal linear inductor to the circuit QED toolkit would be very useful, it is challenging to realize.
elements.sent-2411-018: Several approaches have already been utilized to approximate an ideal inductor, often in pursuit of making a superinductor\,\cite{Manucharyan_2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, these approaches might not be optimal for building inductively shunted circuits with lower impedances.
elements.sent-2411-020: One approach leverages the geometric inductance of superconducting wires to build inductors that are highly linear\,\cite{Peruzzo_2020,Peruzzo_2021,Hassani2023 , but often requires the inductor to be several mm long in order to achieve a typical inductance of a few nH.}
elements.sent-2411-021: This can introduce higher-order modes in the operational frequency range of the circuit which are often strongly coupled to the modes of interest.
elements.sent-2411-022: Another approach uses arrays of Josephson junctions (JJAs) to achieve a large inductance in a compact geometry\,\cite{Manucharyan_2009,Masluk_2012,Pop2014,Nguyen_2019,Kuzmin2019,Pechenezhskiy2020,Zhang_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, these inductors often have significant non-linearities and can also suffer from low frequency parasitic modes\,\cite{Ferguson_2013,Weissl_2015,Catelani_2015,DiPaolo2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: In both cases, each additional parasitic mode increases the number of spurious interactions that can be accidentally activated during operation.
elements.sent-2411-025: While both approaches used to realize a lumped-element, linear and low-loss inductor have limitations, a promising alternative approach is to utilize the high kinetic inductance in granular or disordered superconductors\,\cite{Annunziata_2010,ohya_room_2014,bruno_reducing_2015,dupre_tunable_2017,shearrow_atomic_2018,joshi_strong_2022,Frasca_NbN_2023,bahr_improving_2024,charpentier2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In particular, granular Aluminum (grAl) is an interesting candidate for this application as it can provide large sheet inductances of $\geq 2\,\mathrm{nH / sq}$\,\cite{grunhaupt_loss_2018,zhang_microresonators_2019,kristen_random_2023 , on par with lithographically defined JJAs.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The microstructure of grAl consists of pure grains of aluminum (Al) that are separated by thin barriers of aluminum oxide\,\cite{Cohen_1968,Deutscher1973,Deutscher_1973_PII,yang_microscopic_2020 and can be modeled as an network of effective Josephson junctions (Fig.\,\ref{Fig1 b, \ref{Fig1 c) \,\cite{maleeva_circuit_2018 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Previous works have already shown that grAl can be used to make inductors with large inductances and low non-linearity\,\cite{maleeva_circuit_2018,grunhaupt_granular_2019,rieger_granular_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, single-photon quality factors of grAl resonators measured so far have been limited to $3\times10^5$ \cite{rotzinger_aluminium-oxide_2017, grunhaupt_loss_2018, maleeva_circuit_2018, zhang_microresonators_2019, borisov_superconducting_2020, he_lumped_2021 , precluding the use of grAl inductors in state-of-the-art circuits that require quality factors beyond $10^6$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this work, we use $91 \pm 1 $ nm thick grAl films to build lumped-element linear inductors that are compatible with high coherence quantum circuits.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our smallest inductor, which is only 2 $\si{\micro \metre}$ wide and 30 $\si{\micro \metre}$ long, achieves an inductance of several nH and is fabricated using photolithography.
elements.sent-2411-032: To bound the non-linearity and microwave loss of grAl films of different sheet inductances, we characterize lumped-element resonators built by shunting the grAl inductors with in-plane capacitors.
elements.sent-2411-033: Our resonators have self-Kerr non-linearities of $0.2 - 20\,\mathrm{Hz/photon}$ indicating that the grAl inductors are much more linear than lithographically defined JJAs of similar size.
elements.sent-2411-034: Moreover, we demonstrate that our best resonators can have quality factors $> 3 \times × 10^6$, surpassing previously reported values for grAl resonators by an order of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-035: Further, we show that the grAl inductor can be integrated with other superconductors like Al or Ta to form the shunt capacitor, where the films are fabricated in separate steps and connected using an Al bandage layer.
elements.sent-2411-036: Our fabrication method alleviates design constraints that other in-situ fabrication techniques face and enables integration of grAl inductors in a wide variety of circuits.
elements.sent-2411-037: While we do not independently measure the losses associated with the contacts between grAl and the Al bandage, we find that they are sufficiently low loss such that the quality factor of our best hybrid grAl/Ta resonator surpasses $4.5\times 10^6$.
elements.sent-2411-038: We also report on a previously unobserved trend that resonators built from grAl films with higher room-temperature resistivity systematically have higher internal loss.
elements.sent-2411-039: After accounting for the dielectric loss in the substrate, we find that losses in resonators with the lowest resistivity films are comparable to surface losses in standard 3D Al transmons\,\cite{ganjam_surpassing_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Additionally, our combined analysis of the all-grAl and hybrid samples suggests that the increased loss that we observe with film resistivity is in agreement with an increase in conductor losses in the grAl film.
elements.sent-2411-041: For the hybrid samples, the resistivity dependence could also have contributions from losses at the bandage contacts.
elements.sent-2411-042: Finally, to investigate the time stability of our grAl inductors, we monitor the resonance frequency of our devices.
elements.sent-2411-043: Occasionally, we find sudden shifts in the resonance frequencies that we attribute to the creation of excess quasiparticles following a high energy impact.
elements.sent-2411-044: Interestingly, we infer that the excess quasiparticle density relaxes back to its steady state value at time scales $\sim1.2\,\mathrm{ms}$ which are similar to values reported for Al transmons\,\cite{Wang2014 but three orders-of-magnitude faster than timescales previously reported for grAl films with similar resistivity\,\cite{grunhaupt_loss_2018,Henriques_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Moreover, despite the more complicated superconducting gap profile in the hybrid resonators, we find similar relaxation times after an impact.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width =\linewidth]{figures/Figure1_V10.pdf} \caption{Lumped-element inductor made from granular aluminum (grAl). (a) Circuit representation of a lumped-element inductor. The ideal lumped-element inductor for superconducting quantum circuits simultaneously minimizes the footprint, non-linearity and loss at microwave frequencies. (b) False-color coded optical microscope image of a lumped-element inductor with $L_{\mathrm{k}} \approx 7.8\,$nH, realized with a thin-film of high-kinetic inductance grAl (red). The inductor is galvanically connected to an Al film (blue) through a bandage layer (yellow). In superconducting grAl, crystalline Al grains are separated by non-stoichiometric $\mathrm{AlO}_x$, resulting in a 3D array of JJs. (c) Effective circuit model of the grAl inductor inspired by the granular microstructure. Due to the large aspect ratio, the grAl strip is modelled as a 1D array of effective JJs. Each effective junction of size $a$ contributes with a kinetic inductance $L_{\mathrm{J}}$ and a capacitance $C_{\mathrm{J}}$, alongside with a small geometric inductance $L_{\mathrm{g,eff}}$ and a capacitance to ground $C_0$. } \label{Fig1}
elements.sent-2411-046: Our strategy for quantifying the intrinsic loss in grAl inductors is to build lumped-element resonators entirely out of grAl and characterize them in a high-Q microwave environment.
elements.sent-2411-047: The resonator design is inspired by the 3D transmon geometry\,\cite{paik2011observation which minimizes participation of the resonance mode in lossy dielectric interfaces ($p_{\mathrm{surf \sim 10^{-4}$) \cite{wang2015surface .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Further, the resonators are housed in a high-purity Al package that has four cylindrical tunnel waveguides.
elements.sent-2411-049: Since the resonator frequencies ($4 – 8\,\mathrm{GHz}$) are well below the waveguide cut-off frequency, they are insensitive to losses at the package seams\,\cite{Axline_2016, lei2023characterization, ganjam_surpassing_2024 (see App.\,\ref{appendix_package_loss ).}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Consequently, the total internal loss of the resonators is dominated by losses associated with the grAl thin films.
elements.sent-2411-051: In a typical 3D transmon, a single JJ provides a few nH of inductance and is shunted by a large capacitor to realize an operational frequency of a few GHz and an impedance of a few hundred ohm.
elements.sent-2411-052: Our resonators are a linear version of the transmon design with similar frequencies and impedances.
elements.sent-2411-053: Instead of a JJ, the inductance of these resonators primarily comes from a strip of grAl.
elements.sent-2411-054: As shown in Fig.\,\ref{Fig_sample_design a, each resonator consists of a narrow strip of width $w_{\mathrm{strip = 2 - 10\,\si{\micro \metre}$ and length $l_{\mathrm{strip = 30 - 500\,\si{\micro \metre}$ made from grAl.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The strip is connected via wide leads (width = $50\,\si{\micro \metre}$) to a pair of electrodes ($0.5\,$mm $\times$ $1\,$mm) forming the shunt capacitor.
elements.sent-2411-056: The length of the leads is adjusted to maintain a minimum separation of $300\,\si{\micro \metre}$ between the electrodes for each strip length.
elements.sent-2411-057: The total inductance of the resonator is dominated by the kinetic inductance $L_{\mathrm{k }$ of the grAl film.}
elements.sent-2411-058: The strip contributes a kinetic inductance $L^{\mathrm{strip _{\mathrm{k = L_{\mathrm{sq N_{\mathrm{sq }$ where $L_{\mathrm{sq }$ is the sheet inductance of the grAl film and $N_{\mathrm{sq = l_{\mathrm{strip /w_{\mathrm{strip }$ is the number of squares in the strip.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The contribution of the strip to $L_{\mathrm{k }$ is significantly larger than the pads or leads.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Thus, the kinetic inductance can be varied by modifying $N_{\mathrm{sq }$ or $L_{\mathrm{sq }$ of the grAl film.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: The frequency of the lumped-element resonator is determined by ${\omega_r = 1/\sqrt{(L_{\mathrm{k + L_{\mathrm{g )C_{\mathrm{s }$ where $C_{\mathrm{s }$ is the total capacitance and $L_{\mathrm{g }$ is the stray geometric inductance.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: For the all-grAl samples in this study, the entire resonator including the capacitor electrodes and leads are fabricated in a single photolithography step using direct laser writing (Heidelberg MLA 150).
elements.sent-2411-063: Each grAl film is deposited by zero-angle evaporation of pure Al (5N) in an oxygen environment, followed by liftoff.
elements.sent-2411-064: Adjusting the partial pressure of the oxygen in the chamber changes the normal-state resistivity $\rho_{\mathrm{n }$ of the grAl film (see App.\,\ref{appendix_fab ).}}
elements.sent-2411-065: This in turn allows tuning of the films’s $L_{\mathrm{sq }$ which is related to $\rho_{\mathrm{n }$ by the Mattis-Bardeen theory\,\cite{mattis1958theory .}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The resonators are fabricated on unannealed 2$^{\prime\prime}$ EFG sapphire substrates.
elements.sent-2411-067: This substrate type has been previously characterized to have a bulk dielectric loss tangent of $(26.6\pm6.9) \times 10^{-8}$\,\cite{ganjam_surpassing_2024, read_precision_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: This results in bulk quality factors of $\sim 4.5 \times 10^6$ for our resonator geometries.
elements.sent-2411-069: For microwave characterization, the diced chips ($\sim 4 \times 20 \,\mathrm{mm}$) are clamped on one end using beryllium-copper leaf-springs and loaded into cylindrical tunnel waveguides made from superconducting high purity (5N5) aluminum as shown in Fig.\,\ref{Fig_sample_design b.}
elements.sent-2411-070: To characterize the resonance frequency $\omega_r$, self-Kerr $K$ and internal loss $Q_{\mathrm{int ^{-1}$ of these lumped-element resonators, we capacitively couple the resonators to a common feedline and perform transmission measurements in a hanger configuration (Fig.\,\ref{Fig_sample_design c).}}}

elements.sent-2411-155: Here we alleviate these constraints by using an ex-situ contact between grAl and Al while using only zero-angle deposition in combination with photolithography.
elements.sent-2411-156: To test this contact, we build hybrid lumped-element resonators that have the same geometry as the all-grAl samples described earlier, but with capacitor electrodes made from Al (Fig.\,\ref{Fig_hybrid a).}
elements.sent-2411-157: We use a multi-step lithography process to fabricate these hybrid resonators.

elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width = 0.85\linewidth]{figures/combined_impacts_v4.pdf} \caption{QP time dynamics after high energy impact: (a) Frequency response of an all-grAl resonator ($f_{\mathrm{r}} =$ 4.60\,GHz, $l_{\mathrm{strip}} =60\,\si{\micro\metre}$, $w_{\mathrm{strip}} =2\, \si{\micro\metre}$, $\rho_{\mathrm{n}}\sim 4160\,\si{\micro\Omega}$cm, $Q_{\mathrm{c}}\sim 400$) following a high-energy impact event measured at $\overline{n}_{\mathrm{ph}} \sim 10^5$. elements.sent-2411-218: While the trapping mechanisms at play need further investigation, this observation proves that interfacing grAl with a high-gap superconductor using an Al bandage need not impact the QP dynamics negatively.
elements.sent-2411-219: Consequently, our results emphasize that long QP lifetimes are not the inevitable fate of grAl inductors.
elements.sent-2411-220: In summary, using the high kinetic inductance of grAl, we have realized an inductor that is simultaneously lumped-element, linear and low-loss.
elements.sent-2411-221: We have fabricated $\sim 90\,\mathrm{nm}$ thick grAl films with sheet inductances of $30 - 320$ pH/sq to implement grAl inductors with several nH of inductance.
elements.sent-2411-222: To validate their utility in high-coherence circuits, we have characterized a total of 20 all-grAl resonators.
elements.sent-2411-223: Their low self-Kerr non-linearity ranging between 0.2 and 20\,Hz/photon makes them significantly more linear than conventional arrays of Josephson junctions of similar length.
elements.sent-2411-224: We find that our data is well described by an effective circuit model that treats the grAl inductor as a 1D array of effective JJs.
elements.sent-2411-225: We estimate the size of these effective junctions to be around 10 - 30\,nm, corresponding to a large number of JJs even for micrometer sized elements.
elements.sent-2411-226: Further, by taking advantage of the 3D transmon geometry and the low loss environment provided by our coaxial tunnel package, we demonstrate internal quality factors that are well beyond $10^{6}$.
elements.sent-2411-227: Since our measurements are primarily sensitive to losses related to the grAl film and the substrate, we uncover a correlation between the normal-state resistivity of grAl and microwave losses.
elements.sent-2411-228: This implies a trade-off between quality factor and resistivity of the grAl film, suggesting that resistivity is an important design consideration for grAl lumped-element inductors and grAl devices in general.
elements.sent-2411-229: A similar degradation of the internal quality factor in the single-photon regime with increasing sheet resistance has also been observed in some other high kinetic inductance materials like NbN and amorphous InO\,\cite{Frasca_NbN_2023,charpentier2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-230: Interestingly, our analysis indicates that our lowest resistivity grAl resonators have surface losses comparable to 3D Al transmons.
elements.sent-2411-231: Furthermore, we have demonstrated high quality factors in hybrid resonators that integrate these high-impedance grAl inductors with low-impedance Al or Ta capacitors.
elements.sent-2411-232: Our method of using a dedicated Al bandage layer to make the contact allows grAl to be compatible with a variety of fabrication processes.
elements.sent-2411-233: Since we observe the same trend with resistivity in all devices, we suspect that increasing losses are caused by increasing conductor losses in the grAl strip.
elements.sent-2411-234: These findings motivate future studies that can uncover the microscopic mechanisms that contribute to losses in grAl films and the contacts in hybrid devices.
elements.sent-2411-235: Finally, we measure millisecond timescales for QP relaxation in our all-grAl resonators.
elements.sent-2411-236: This is significantly shorter than previous reports on grAl resonators, but comparable to lifetimes measured in Al transmons with similar geometry.
elements.sent-2411-237: Our results merit further investigation into the role of QP localization in grAl films as a function of resistivity and thickness, and how it affects the measured lifetime.

elements.sent-2411-256: All resonators were fabricated on sapphire substrates grown using the edge-fed film growth (EFG) method and patterned using photolithography.
elements.sent-2411-257: For the all-grAl devices, the wafers were first solvent cleaned by sonication in N-Methylpyrrolidone (NMP), acetone and ispropanol, followed by a DI water rinse.
elements.sent-2411-258: The wafers were then dehydrated and primed with hexamethyldisilizane (HMDS) in a YES-310TA Vapor Prime Oven and immediately coated with a bilayer photoresist stack of LOR 5A and S1805.
elements.sent-2411-259: The exposure was done using a Heidelberg MLA 150 Direct Writer.
elements.sent-2411-260: After a 75 sec development in Microposit MF319 developer, the wafers were treated with oxygen plasma using an AutoGlow 200 at 100\,W, 300\,mTorr for 2 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-261: To deposit the grAl film, the wafer was then loaded into a Plassys electron-beam evaporator, where aluminum was evaporated at zero angle while flowing oxygen and rotating the wafer at $\sim25$\,deg/sec.
elements.sent-2411-262: The corresponding oxygen partial pressure in the chamber was $\sim 5 \times 10^{-5}$\,Torr and the Al was evaporated at 1\,nm/sec.
elements.sent-2411-263: The film was lifted off in a hot NMP bath at $\sim 90\,^{\circ}$C for a few hours followed by rinsing in NMP, acetone, isopropanol and DI water.
elements.sent-2411-264: The resistivity of the grAl film was then probed using four-probe van der Pauw measurements.
elements.sent-2411-265: The measured room-temperature resistivity of the films is plotted vs the corresponding oxygen flow rate used during evaporation in Fig.\,\ref{fig_rho_vs_O2 .}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width =\linewidth]{figures/appendix_rho_vs_O2.pdf} \caption{Room-temperature resistivity of measured samples vs oxygen flow used rate while evaporating Al. The corresponding oxygen partial pressure in the chamber was $\sim 5 \times 10^{-5}\,$ mbar. Different colors and symbols correspond to different wafers. These wafers were fabricated using Al from the same crucible without refilling between runs.} \label{fig_rho_vs_O2}
elements.sent-2411-266: For the hybrid grAl/Al samples, the grAl strip was fabricated as described above.
elements.sent-2411-267: The 80\,nm thick Al pads were fabricated in a second round of patterning following the same steps.
elements.sent-2411-268: The bandage layer was fabricated in a third round of patterning also following the same procedure but with an additional thin layer ($< 100\,$nm) of 495 PMMA A3 spun before spinning the photoresist stack.
elements.sent-2411-269: The PMMA was used to protect the pre-patterned grAl and Al features from being etched by the MF319 developer.
elements.sent-2411-270: After development, the PMMA layer was removed by oxygen plasma in the AutoGlow 200 at 100\,W, 300\,mtorr for 3 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-271: Additionally, an in-situ Ar ion beam milling at 400\,V, 15\,mA, 4\,sccm was done for 4 min to remove the oxide layers prior to the Al bandage deposition.
elements.sent-2411-272: For the hybrid grAl/Ta samples, the EFG sapphire substrates were first cleaned in a piranha solution (2:1 H$_2$SO$_4$:H$_2$O$_2$) and annealed at 1200\,$^{\circ}$C in an oxygen-rich environment.
elements.sent-2411-273: Then 75\,nm of Ta was deposited on the entire wafer by DC magnetron sputtering at an elevated temperature of 800\,$^{\circ}$C using an Ar pressure of 6\,mTorr at 2.5\,$\mathrm{\AA}$/sec.
elements.sent-2411-274: The Ta film was then coated with S1827 and the photoresist was patterned using photolithography, followed by development in MF319.
elements.sent-2411-275: The wafer was then hard-baked for 1 min at 120\,$^{\circ}$C and oxygen plasma cleaned in the AutoGlow 200 at 150\,W, 300\,mTorr for 2 mins to remove resist residue.
elements.sent-2411-276: The Ta was then etched at a rate of 100\,nm/min in an Oxford 80+ Reactive Ion Etcher using SF$_6$ with a flow rate of 20\,sccm, a pressure of 10\,mTorr, and an RF power of 50\,W.
elements.sent-2411-277: After etching, the wafer was sonicated in NMP, acetone, isopropanol and DI water to remove the photoresist.
elements.sent-2411-278: The wafer was then piranha cleaned again and the Ta oxide was stripped using Transene 10:1 BOE for 20 mins.
elements.sent-2411-279: This was followed by patterning of the grAl strip and Al bandage layers using the procedure described earlier.
elements.sent-2411-280: After device fabrication, all wafers were coated with protective resist and diced using an ADT ProVectus 7100 dicer followed by a cleaning in NMP, acetone, isopropanol and DI water.
elements.sent-2411-281: A summary of the grAl film parameters, resonator dimensions, measured frequencies and quality factors of all the samples is given in Table \ref{table_info .}
elements.sent-2411-282: The RF wiring setup used for characterizing the resonators is shown in Fig.\,\ref{fig_fridge_wiring .}
elements.sent-2411-283: The resonators are housed in an Al package which is mounted to the mixing chamber stage of a dilution refrigerator.
elements.sent-2411-284: The input and output ports of the package use custom CR-110 IR Eccosorb low-pass filters to reduce the exposure of our samples to pair-breaking infrared radiation.
elements.sent-2411-285: The package and Eccosorb filters are placed within a MuMetal can to screen external magnetic fields.
elements.sent-2411-286: Both, input and output lines, are filtered with 12 GHz K\&L low-pass filters.
elements.sent-2411-287: Across all stages, the input line has approximately 90\,dB attenuation in the 4-8 GHz range based on room temperature calibration.
elements.sent-2411-288: There is 20\,dB of attenuation at the 4K stage.
elements.sent-2411-289: The remaining 70\,dB of attenuation is at the mixing chamber stage, with 20\,dB of that attenuation achieved by a directional coupler similar to Ref.\,\cite{ganjam_surpassing_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-290: The first amplifier stage is a high-electron-mobility transistor (HEMT) amplifier anchored at the 4 K stage.
elements.sent-2411-291: At room temperature, there is an additional MITEQ amplifier before the output signal is detected by a vector network analyzer (VNA).
elements.sent-2411-292: All frequency and time-domain measurements were performed using an Agilent E5071C VNA which was clocked to an SRS FS725 10\,MHz Rb frequency standard.

elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width =\linewidth]{figures/two_impact_circle_gral_4.png} \caption{Complex response of an all-grAl resonator during a high energy impact: The time trace data (grey markers) for the all-grAl resonator from Fig. \,\ref{Fig_impacts} ($f_{\mathrm{r}} =$ 4.60\,GHz, $l_{\mathrm{strip}} =60\,\si{\micro\metre}$, $w_{\mathrm{strip}} =2\, \si{\micro\metre}$, $\rho_{\mathrm{n}}\sim 4160\,\si{\micro\Omega}$cm, $Q_{\mathrm{c}}\sim 400$) is plotted on top of its corresponding resonance circle (red). The kinetic inductance fraction $\alpha = 1 - L_{\mathrm{g}}C_{\mathrm{s}}\omega_{\mathrm{r}}^2$ is estimated from the measured resonance frequency $f_{\mathrm{r}}$ and the simulated geometric inductance $L_{\mathrm{g}}$ and shunt capacitance $C_{\mathrm{s}}$. The internal quality factor $Q_{\mathrm{int}}$ is given in the single-photon regime for all samples that have a reasonable ratio of internal to coupling quality factor. } \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c c|c c
